title: About
text: This site provides real-time monitoring and nowcasting of recession risk across the Euro Area and its main member states. The project is an empirical application and extension of the Bayesian nowcasting framework introduced by Furno and Giannone (2024).

title: About
text:
Data Sources
The model uses public macroeconomic and financial data retrieved directly from the APIs of official European statistical and financial institutions:

Gross Domestic Product (GDP)

Source: Eurostat API (Dataset: namq_10_gdp).
Definition: Quarter-on-Quarter percentage change of seasonally and calendar adjusted real Gross Domestic Product (market prices, B1GQ).
Role: Target variable for labeling technical recessions (defined as two consecutive quarters of negative growth).

Economic Sentiment Indicator (ESI)

Source: Eurostat API (Dataset: ei_bssi_m_r2).
Definition: Monthly composite index representing sentiment across industry, services, consumers, retail trade, and construction. The quarterly value is the simple average of the monthly indices in the quarter.
Role: Macroeconomic indicator representing economic activity expectations.

Composite Indicator of Systemic Stress (CISS)

Source: European Central Bank (ECB) Data Portal (Key: CISS.M..Z0Z.4F.EC.SS_CIN.B for the monthly aggregate index).
Definition: A monthly index measuring systemic stress in the financial system. It aggregates segment-specific stress indices using portfolio theory to weight segments by their correlation, emphasizing joint stress events. The quarterly value is the average of the monthly CISS values in the quarter.
Role: Financial stress indicator representing capital and credit risks.

title: About
text:
Model and Computation
The project is implemented in R and compiled via Quarto. The core statistical modeling relies on:

Bayesian Modeling: Estimated via Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) using the brms package, which runs on top of Stan.
Hierarchical Pooling: Intercepts and indicator coefficients (slopes) are modeled with group-level random effects grouped by geography (geo). This pools informational strength across countries to make predictions more robust.
Replicability: The R script recession-index.R downloads the latest data, fits the model, and saves the data. The site compiles dynamically to display updated probabilities.

title: Methodology
text: This document describes the econometric and statistical methodology used to nowcast recession risk in the Euro Area and its four main member states: Germany (DE), France (FR), Italy (IT), and Spain (ES). The framework is based on the methodology proposed by Furno and Giannone (2024), extended here to a hierarchical multi-country setting.

title: Methodology
text:
1 The Nowcasting Problem
A central challenge in macroeconomic monitoring is the timely detection of business cycle turning points. Dating committees, such as the National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER) in the United States and the Centre for Economic Policy Research (CEPR) in the Euro Area, officially announce recessions with a delay of several months or even years. Official quarterly Gross Domestic Product (GDP) figures are also published with a lag of several weeks and are subject to subsequent revisions.
To address these limitations, we formulate a model to nowcast the probability that a country is in a recession during the current quarter. Unlike standard forecasting models that predict future recessions over a horizon \(h > 0\) (e.g., Estrella and Mishkin (1998)), nowcasting focuses on estimating the state of the economy at the present moment \(h = 0\) (see Giannone, Reichlin, and Small (2008) and Bańbura et al. (2013)).

title: Methodology
text:
2 Model Specification
Let \(y_{i,t} \in \{0, 1\}\) be a binary indicator representing whether geography \(i\) is in a recession at quarter \(t\). The probability of recession is denoted by: \[ p_{i,t} = P(y_{i,t} = 1 \mid \mathbf{x}_{i,t}) \]
We model \(y_{i,t}\) using a Hierarchical Bayesian Logit regression. The log-odds (logit) of being in a recession is a linear function of a macroeconomic indicator and a financial indicator:
\[ \text{logit}(p_{i,t}) = \ln\left(\frac{p_{i,t}}{1 - p_{i,t}}\right) = \alpha_i + \beta_{1,i} \text{CISS}_{i,t} + \beta_{2,i} \text{ESI}_{i,t} \]
where: - \(\text{CISS}_{i,t}\) is the standardized Composite Indicator of Systemic Stress for geography \(i\). - \(\text{ESI}_{i,t}\) is the standardized Economic Sentiment Indicator for geography \(i\).

2.1 Hierarchical Structure
A key feature of our approach is the hierarchical pooling of parameters across countries. Rather than estimating independent models for each country or a single pooled model that assumes identical relationships, the hierarchical model assumes that country-specific parameters are drawn from a shared global distribution:
\[ \alpha_i = \alpha_0 + u_{0,i} \] \[ \beta_{1,i} = \beta_1 + u_{1,i} \] \[ \beta_{2,i} = \beta_2 + u_{2,i} \]
where: - \(\alpha_0\) represents the global average intercept. - \(\beta_1\) and \(\beta_2\) represent the global average effects of the financial stress indicator (CISS) and macroeconomic sentiment (ESI), respectively. - \(\mathbf{u}_i = (u_{0,i}, u_{1,i}, u_{2,i})'\) is a vector of geography-specific random effects.
We assume that the country-specific deviations are normally distributed: \[ \mathbf{u}_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{\Sigma}) \]
The covariance matrix \(\mathbf{\Sigma}\) captures the variability of the intercepts and slopes across geographies, as well as the correlations between them: \[ \mathbf{\Sigma} = \text{diag}(\boldsymbol{\sigma}) \, \mathbf{R} \, \text{diag}(\boldsymbol{\sigma}) \] where \(\boldsymbol{\sigma} = (\sigma_{\alpha}, \sigma_{\beta_1}, \sigma_{\beta_2})'\) are the standard deviations of the random effects, and \(\mathbf{R}\) is the correlation matrix.
This hierarchical structure allows for “shrinkage,” which pulls country-specific estimates toward the Euro Area average. This is particularly beneficial for countries with short sample histories or fewer recession observations, as the model borrows statistical power from the rest of the group.

title: Methodology
text:
3 Data and Variables

3.1 Target Variable: Technical Recession
Following standard convention, we define a technical recession as two consecutive quarters of negative quarter-on-quarter real GDP growth:
\[ y_{i,t} = \begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } \Delta \text{GDP}_{i,t} < 0 \text{ and } \Delta \text{GDP}_{i,t-1} < 0 \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases} \]
where \(\Delta \text{GDP}_{i,t}\) represents the percentage growth rate of seasonally and calendar adjusted real GDP for country \(i\) in quarter \(t\).


3.2 Predictor Variables

Economic Sentiment Indicator (ESI): Sourced from Eurostat, the ESI is a composite indicator made up of five sectoral confidence indicators with different weights (industrial, services, consumer, construction, and retail trade). It serves as a timely tracker of macroeconomic sentiment.
Composite Indicator of Systemic Stress (CISS): Developed by Holló, Kremer, and Lo Duca (2012) and sourced from the European Central Bank, the CISS measures the level of systemic stress in the financial system. It aggregates indicators across five market segments (money, equity, bond, foreign exchange, and financial intermediaries) using portfolio theory to weight segments by their correlation. This captures the systemic dimension of financial stress, which has been shown to be a crucial driver of economic downside risks (Adrian, Boyarchenko, and Giannone (2019)).

Prior to estimation, both predictors are scaled (mean-centered and divided by their standard deviation) to facilitate MCMC convergence.

title: Methodology
text:
4 Bayesian Estimation and Priors
The model is estimated using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods via the brms package in R, which interface with Stan. We specify weakly informative priors for the parameters to stabilize estimation while letting the data dominate the posterior:

4.1 Population-Level (Fixed) Effects

Global Intercept: \(\alpha_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 5)\)
Global Slopes: \(\beta_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 2)\) for \(k \in \{1, 2\}\)

These priors are conservative; they restrict coefficients to plausible ranges while remaining flat enough over the regions of high likelihood.


4.2 Group-Level (Random) Effects

Standard deviations: \(\sigma \sim \text{Student-}t(3, 0, 2.5)\) (truncated to positive values)
Correlation matrix: \(\mathbf{R} \sim \text{LKJ}(1)\)

The LKJ prior with \(\eta = 1\) is uniform over all correlation matrices, representing a neutral prior belief about the correlations between random intercepts and slopes.

title: Methodology
text:
5 Inference and Out-of-Sample Evaluation
The model is fitted on training data up to a cutoff date representing the maximum available data minus 5 years. This allows us to perform out-of-sample inference on the last 5 years.
For each quarter \(t\), we compute the posterior probability distribution of \(p_{i,t}\). The central estimate is the posterior mean: \[ \hat{p}_{i,t} = \mathbb{E}[p_{i,t} \mid \text{Data}] \] We also construct 95% Bayesian credible intervals using the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles of the posterior distribution to represent model uncertainty.

title: Euro Area Recession Risk Indicator
text:
Welcome to the Euro Area Recession Risk Tracker. This site presents real-time nowcasts of recession probabilities for the Euro Area (EA) and its four largest member states: Germany (DE), France (FR), Italy (IT), and Spain (ES).
Our methodology builds on the Bayesian logit nowcasting framework proposed by Furno and Giannone (2024). By utilizing a Hierarchical Bayesian model, we allow the model to share information across different regions. This hierarchical structure (“shrinkage”) is particularly valuable for improving the reliability of country-specific nowcasts during periods of high economic uncertainty.
Code
# Install packages if missing
# install.packages(c("eurostat", "ecb", "dplyr", "tidyr", "lubridate", 
#                    "ggplot2", "patchwork", "brms", "zoo"))

library(eurostat)
library(ecb)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(lubridate)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
library(brms)
library(zoo)

# Target countries: Euro Area (EA), Germany (DE), France (FR), Italy (IT), Spain (ES)
# Note: Eurostat uses 'EA' or 'EA19'/'EA20', ECB uses 'U2' for the Euro Area.
target_geo_eurostat <- c("EA20", "EA19", "DE", "FR", "IT", "ES")
target_geo_ecb <- c("U2", "DE", "FR", "IT", "ES")

## 1. Download GDP Data (Eurostat)

## 1. Download GDP Data (Eurostat)

# 'namq_10_gdp' is the Eurostat dataset for GDP and main components
gdp_raw <- get_eurostat("namq_10_gdp", time_format = "date", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

gdp <- gdp_raw %>%
  # Safely rename new API columns to the old names, ignoring them if they don't exist
  rename(any_of(c(time = "TIME_PERIOD", values = "OBS_VALUE"))) %>%
  filter(
    na_item == "B1GQ",         # Gross domestic product at market prices
    s_adj == "SCA",            # Seasonally and calendar adjusted data
    unit == "CLV_PCH_PRE",         # Percentage change on previous period (QoQ)
    geo %in% target_geo_eurostat
  ) %>%
  select(geo, time, gdp_growth = values) %>%
  mutate(
    # Standardize Euro Area code to 'EA'
    geo = ifelse(grepl("EA", geo), "EA", geo),
    quarter = as.yearqtr(time)
  ) %>%
  # In case of overlapping EA definitions, keep the longest non-NA series
  group_by(geo, quarter) %>%
  summarise(gdp_growth = mean(gdp_growth, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# Downlaod CISS Data
# The CISS key structure: CISS.Frequency.ReferenceArea...
# Using wildcard for the country/area to get all available
ciss_key <- "CISS.D..Z0Z.4F.EC.SS_CIN.IDX"
ciss_raw <- get_data(ciss_key)

ciss <- ciss_raw %>%
  filter(ref_area %in% target_geo_ecb) %>%
  mutate(
    obstime_char = as.character(obstime),
    time = as.Date(ifelse(nchar(obstime_char) == 7, paste0(obstime_char, "-01"), obstime_char)),
    geo = ifelse(ref_area == "U2", "EA", ref_area),
    quarter = as.yearqtr(time)
  ) %>%
  group_by(geo, quarter) %>%
  summarise(ciss = mean(obsvalue, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

## 3. Download Economic Sentiment Indicator (Eurostat)

esi_raw <- get_eurostat("ei_bssi_m_r2", time_format = "date", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

esi <- esi_raw %>%
  # Safely rename new API columns to the old names, ignoring them if they don't exist
  rename(any_of(c(time = "TIME_PERIOD", values = "OBS_VALUE"))) %>%
  filter(
    indic == "BS-ESI-I",       # Economic Sentiment Indicator
    s_adj == "SA",             # Seasonally adjusted
    geo %in% target_geo_eurostat
  ) %>%
  select(geo, time, esi = values) %>%
  mutate(
    geo = ifelse(grepl("EA", geo), "EA", geo),
    quarter = as.yearqtr(time)
  ) %>%
  group_by(geo, quarter) %>%
  summarise(esi = mean(esi, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# Combine data and calculate recession risk
# Merge all datasets
df <- gdp %>%
  inner_join(ciss, by = c("geo", "quarter")) %>%
  inner_join(esi, by = c("geo", "quarter")) %>%
  arrange(geo, quarter) %>%
  group_by(geo) %>%
  mutate(
    # Recession definition: current quarter < 0 AND previous quarter < 0
    recession = ifelse(gdp_growth < 0 & lag(gdp_growth, 1) < 0, 1, 0)
  ) %>%
  drop_na() %>% # Drop NAs created by lag and missing series overlaps
  ungroup()

# Standardize the predictors to help the Bayesian model converge faster
df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    ciss_scaled = scale(ciss)[,1],
    esi_scaled = scale(esi)[,1]
  )

# Hierarchical Bayesian Logit model
# Determine the cut-off date (5 years ago from the maximum available date in the dataset)
max_date <- max(df$quarter)
cutoff_date <- max_date - 5 # 5 years subtraction on yearqtr object

# Split data
train_data <- df %>% filter(quarter <= cutoff_date)
inference_data <- df # We infer on the whole set

# Hierarchical Bayesian Logit Model using brms
# Random intercepts and random slopes for each geo (Euro Area and countries)
formula <- brmsformula(
  recession ~ ciss_scaled + esi_scaled + (1 + ciss_scaled + esi_scaled | geo),
  family = bernoulli(link = "logit")
)

# Fit the model (this may take a minute or two depending on your machine)
# We set a seed for reproducibility. 
brms_model <- brm(
  formula = formula,
  data = train_data,
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(0, 5), class = "Intercept"),
    prior(normal(0, 2), class = "b")
  ),
  chains = 2,
  cores = 2,
  iter = 2000,
  seed = 123,
  silent = 2,
  refresh = 0
)

summary(brms_model)


 Family: bernoulli 
  Links: mu = logit 
Formula: recession ~ ciss_scaled + esi_scaled + (1 + ciss_scaled + esi_scaled | geo) 
   Data: train_data (Number of observations: 572) 
  Draws: 2 chains, each with iter = 2000; warmup = 1000; thin = 1;
         total post-warmup draws = 2000

Multilevel Hyperparameters:
~geo (Number of levels: 5) 
                            Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS
sd(Intercept)                   1.04      0.67     0.08     2.75 1.00      433
sd(ciss_scaled)                 0.37      0.34     0.01     1.26 1.01      355
sd(esi_scaled)                  0.54      0.58     0.02     2.70 1.00      420
cor(Intercept,ciss_scaled)      0.04      0.49    -0.85     0.88 1.01     1863
cor(Intercept,esi_scaled)      -0.13      0.49    -0.91     0.82 1.00     1710
cor(ciss_scaled,esi_scaled)     0.08      0.51    -0.86     0.91 1.00     1271
                            Tail_ESS
sd(Intercept)                    387
sd(ciss_scaled)                 1079
sd(esi_scaled)                   164
cor(Intercept,ciss_scaled)      1201
cor(Intercept,esi_scaled)       1159
cor(ciss_scaled,esi_scaled)     1426

Regression Coefficients:
            Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
Intercept      -3.39      0.60    -4.75    -2.12 1.00      618      779
ciss_scaled     0.64      0.27     0.11     1.26 1.00      792      387
esi_scaled     -1.41      0.34    -2.05    -0.71 1.00      637      300

Draws were sampled using sampling(NUTS). For each parameter, Bulk_ESS
and Tail_ESS are effective sample size measures, and Rhat is the potential
scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence, Rhat = 1).


Code
# OUt of sample inference
# Extract predicted probabilities (Estimate = mean of posterior)
predictions <- fitted(brms_model, newdata = inference_data, type = "response")

df_results <- inference_data %>%
  mutate(
    prob_recession = predictions[, "Estimate"],
    lower_95 = predictions[, "Q2.5"],
    upper_95 = predictions[, "Q97.5"],
    is_forecast = ifelse(quarter > cutoff_date, "Inferred (Last 5 Yrs)", "Training Data")
  )

title: Euro Area Recession Risk Indicator
text:
Dashboard Overview
This interactive dashboard displays the estimated recession probabilities derived from our Hierarchical Bayesian Logit model. By combining macroeconomic sentiment (ESI) and financial conditions (CISS) within a unified hierarchical structure, the model estimates the probability that the Euro Area and its four largest economies are in a technical recession.

How to Read the Charts

Recession Probability (Solid Line): Represents the posterior mean probability of being in a recession.
95% Credible Intervals (Shaded Ribbons): Reflect the uncertainty in our estimates. The wider the ribbon, the more uncertainty there is in the estimate.
Historical Recessions (Grey Shaded Bars): Indicate periods of actual recessions defined by the technical definition of two consecutive quarters of negative quarter-on-quarter GDP growth.
Data Splitting (Red Dashed Line): Denotes the out-of-sample cutoff.

Blue Line/Ribbon (Training Data): In-sample model performance.
Orange Line/Ribbon (Inferred Data): Out-of-sample estimates for the last 5 years, showing how the model performs on unseen data.

title: Euro Area Recession Risk Indicator
text:
Model Estimation Results
Below, we run the visualization routine to plot the estimated probabilities. The top panel displays the results for the aggregate Euro Area, while the bottom panel shows the individual country-level results for Germany, France, Italy, and Spain.


Code
# Data visualization
# Function to generate a plot for a specific geography
plot_geo <- function(data, region_name, is_main = FALSE) {
  
  p_data <- data %>% filter(geo == region_name)
  
  p <- ggplot(p_data, aes(x = as.Date(quarter))) +
    # Actual Recession shaded areas
    geom_rect(
      data = p_data %>% filter(recession == 1),
      aes(xmin = as.Date(quarter) - 45, xmax = as.Date(quarter) + 45, 
          ymin = 0, ymax = 1), 
      fill = "grey80", alpha = 0.5, inherit.aes = FALSE
    ) +
    # Recession Probability line
    geom_line(aes(y = prob_recession, color = is_forecast), size = 1) +
    # 95% Credible Intervals
    geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower_95, ymax = upper_95, fill = is_forecast), alpha = 0.2) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = as.Date(cutoff_date), linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(), limits = c(0, 1)) +
    scale_color_manual(values = c("Training Data" = "#004B87", "Inferred (Last 5 Yrs)" = "#E37222")) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c("Training Data" = "#004B87", "Inferred (Last 5 Yrs)" = "#E37222")) +
    labs(
      title = region_name,
      y = "Recession Probability",
      x = "",
      color = "Data Period",
      fill = "Data Period"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
      legend.position = "bottom",
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = ifelse(is_main, 16, 12))
    )
  
  return(p)
}

# 1. Main plot for Euro Area
p_ea <- plot_geo(df_results, "EA", is_main = TRUE) + 
  labs(subtitle = "Grey bars indicate actual historical recessions (2 consecutive quarters of negative growth). Red dashed line denotes the 5-year data cut-off.")

# 2. Country plots
p_de <- plot_geo(df_results, "DE")
p_fr <- plot_geo(df_results, "FR")
p_it <- plot_geo(df_results, "IT")
p_es <- plot_geo(df_results, "ES")

# Combine country plots into a 2x2 grid
country_grid <- (p_de | p_fr) / (p_it | p_es) + 
  plot_layout(guides = "collect") & 
  theme(legend.position = 'bottom')

# Final layout: EA on top, 2x2 grid below
final_plot <- p_ea / country_grid + plot_layout(heights = c(1, 1.5))

final_plot
